narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 stands out as the most influential factor in the model's assessment, actually working in your favor by suggesting a stronger chance of passing the bar exam compared to what it might have expected, even though it's below the 3.24 average among students who succeeded. However, your position in the 3rd decile for Year 3 grades pushed the prediction toward failure, as this ranking is lower than the typical 5.91 decile seen in passers, signaling potential challenges in sustaining higher performance. Similarly, your LSAT score of 32 contributed toward the failure prediction, falling short of the 37.27 average among those who passed, which the model weighed heavily as a key indicator of readiness. Overall, these top three factors—your undergrad grades helping you, but your later school decile and LSAT pulling against—led the model to estimate a 48.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam on the first try, crossing the threshold that prompted the admission office to reject your application to protect their pass rates. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many successful students, and opting for full-time study matches the norm for passers, but they weren't decisive here. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows solid alignment in background but lags in those critical academic metrics that the model prioritizes most. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—models like this focus narrowly on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to 37 or above, as even a modest gain could shift predictions dramatically. Strengthening your recent academic decile through targeted coursework or grad school prep might also help rebuild that signal. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on bolstering your Year 3 record, and explore programs with holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment and background. With focused improvements, you can reapply stronger next cycle.
